speaker: interviewer
text: And survivor's name is Dolf Ringel. Interviewer is Bonnie [? Dwork. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The mine worker led us through the streets, the darkened streets, of Montceau-les-Mines to a little-- to a house of a mine worker where we were fed, and we rested a few hours. And in the morning, he-- he took out the three of us. And through the-- it must have been maybe 5:30, 6 o'clock in the morning. And we silently went through the town until we reached a ditch. And he said, on the other side is non-occupied France. But now, I will rush up that hill, and when I see that everything is OK, then I will give you a signal, and you follow. And you run as fast as you can, because along the ditch, they usually sit with the machine guns. And anyone crossing, they shoot him. So you run as fast as you can. He-- in a second, he was up that hill, he looked around, waved us. And then we raced over and didn't stop until we reached-- reached him. He embraced us and said in French, you are now in non-occupied France. Mais pase libre-- but not free. Remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we paid him twice as much-- the last money practically we had. Then he asked-- ask very little money. He asked. He embraced us. And we thanked him. And he-- he went back. And we continued walking through tiny little villages until we reached somewhere, a place where we-- where we heard that there is a railroad station. We stayed overnight. Took the train to Lyon, and reached Lyon the next morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we saw a family-- a gentlemen, a woman, and two girls-- coming our way. while we were walking through the streets of Lyon. And we approached them. And in our best French asked them, can you tell us where the Office Nederlander is? This is the Dutch Office, the office of the legitimate Dutch government in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: In his best Dutch, he answered, you can also talk to me in Dutch, because I'm also a refugee from Amsterdam. My name-- my name is [? Reishave. ?] He told us were it was, asked us immediately whether we need money. He had helped-- it turned out that he had helped [? Dottie's ?] brother. We went to the consulate. It was actually not a consulate anymore. It was a Dutch representation.

speaker: interviewee
text: My first question was-- I don't know why. Maybe out of relief, a reaction, I sat to that Vice Consul, can my fiance and I get married here? He started laughing, and said, is that all of you have on your mind? After you saved your life, and you are not free yet, you are not out of the woods yet, that's what you want to do? Wait with it. Live together, he said. But you have other things to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told us, you are now in-- in Vichy France. But it is very dangerous here. The Gestapo is all over. And the French collaborate with the Gestapo. You cannot go to a hotel. With this, a Dutch young woman turned around and said, I'm just leaving for-- for Switzerland. I got-- I have a way to get there. You can have the address where I slept at night. Gave us the address. It was a Jewish furrier. We went there. He took us in. We stayed overnight. And we got bugs all over from the-- from the hides there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of bugs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bed bugs. The worst kind.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Vice Consul heard but I lived in Spain, that I have still Spanish friends, and that I was fluent in Spain, he said, you are much better off if you try to get to Spain than to Switzerland. The Swiss have started to send back refugees. And that is very dangerous. You may-- you may not make it. You are better off going to Spain. So we decided, and we go to Spain. We took the train. Took us the whole night. We arrived in Perpignan. I knew Perpignan. So I knew immediately where to go. We took a hotel for a night. We still have a few-- a few francs left. And I called the Spanish friend the next day. Told him, I'm here in Perpignan. I have fled from Holland. I'm here with my fiance and a friend. Can you help us?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I'm going to come over. I see you. He came a couple of days later. And I still didn't tell him what we wanted. I actually wanted him to give us a smuggler to get over the border, because I had no hope that we would ever get legally a French exit visa and a Spanish entry visa, as young men military age could not get anymore an exit visa from the French. And without an exit visa, you could not get a Spanish visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fortunately, a Dutch Vice Consul in Perpignan, he helped us to obtain temporary residence in Perpignan. And with this, we could obtain coupons to eat. I found a [? forwarder ?] with whom I had business, who took guilders from us and exchanged them in francs. So all of a sudden, we still had money again. And we were able to live in a hotel-- in a small hotel. There, we met Dutch young-- a young Dutch guy. Started talking to him. And he told us-- we asked him, are there other Dutch people here? He said, yes, Rita and Ichu Kolmer. Those were good friends of ours from Amsterdam, from the Hague-- not Amsterdam, from the Hague-- who had fled before we had fled. So we met. Rita had a visa, a valid visa, for leaving-- to leave France, and also Spanish visa. But she didn't want to leave without her husband. So they had only-- they were only married for four month. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we urged her to leave, because there have been rumors that an invasion of North Africa might occur. And nobody knew what the consequences would be if Allies invaded Morocco. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she left. And I had to swear to her that we would not leave Ichu behind. I said, I don't have to swear. He's a friend of mine. I won't leave him behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed for several weeks until the 11th of November came around, 1942, when in the morning on the radio, we heard that American and British troops had landed in Morocco, and that in retaliation, Hitler's troops had crossed non-occupied France, and were-- were occupying the rest of France. Before it came to that date, we had been eating in a restaurant where we had met-- met a German Jew by the name of Kurt Ehrlich. He lived together with the owner, a French widow and owner of this restaurant. And he was very pessimistic. He said, the Germans will come any day. This is not going to remain that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what are you doing then? He said, then I know someone. And he will guide me over the Pyrenees. I said, give me the name, too. We're also-- you know we are-- we are refugees. Help us. He said, OK, I will-- he will be in town in a few days. I introduce you to him. And he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made arrangements with this man that should the moment come, that we give him a telephone call to his house up in [NON-ENGLISH]. That is where he lived, in the Pyrenees already on the French side. And he would then wait for us, and at the station in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: The day that the Germans invaded the non-occupied zone, I asked Ehrlich again, how do we get now to [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, you have to have a sauf-conduit. Sauf-conduit is translated in English, 'safe conduct.' For every travel to a border zone, you needed a-- a safe conduct from the-- from the police to travel the train to that area. And how do I get such a safe conduct? He said you have to have a reason-- health reason. So the four of us-- [? Dottie, ?] Max, Ichu, and I-- we walked to the doctor, who was of the good kind. And he said, so, what's wrong with you people? I said, we all-- we all have maladie de l'estomac-- a-- our-- stomach sickness. He said, yes, apparently an epidemic. I know what you guys are up to. OK, I give you all a medical certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the police. The police cooperated. They knew exactly what we were up to. But most of the police in Perpignan were also anti-German, anti-Nazi. And they gave us the sauf-conduit. [? Dottie ?] still has hers. I don't have mine anymore. The next day, we wanted to leave, when suddenly that young Dutch guy that we had met in our hotel came storming and said, crying, the tears running down his cheeks, he said, don't leave me here, because I'm going to die. Take me along. So I didn't have the heart to say, no, you may not come. So we were now five. We went to an electric train that I knew existed, that most people did not know. And that was a train from Perpignan to Andorra, and from there to the-- to Spain, to the Spanish border. We boarded that train, which fortunately still was running. We went to [NON-ENGLISH]. There the guy-- the guide was standing. And we started with our trek over the Pyrenees, which took two days and two nights. And it was bitter cold. It was so cold. And we didn't have coats for that. We didn't have food other than sugar cubes that he-- the guy had advised us to buy. But they were-- they were gone in no time. We had water, but nothing to eat. And high up in the Pyrenees, he allowed us to rest. My friend, our friend Ichu, who we had met in Perpignan again, he was chubby, and the-- the opposite of a sporty type. Every so often, he fell. And after having climbed for maybe 10 or 12 hours, he couldn't anymore. When he fell again, he said, continue. I can't anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I kicked him in his behind and said, you must come. And [? James ?] got feet. And I got-- helped him under his arms to struggle along. And finally, finally, I don't know how, but it was morning when the guide said, do you see the-- the smoke coming off the chimney on the other side? That's Spain. That belongs to the first village on the Spanish side, Macanet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He left us. We paid him. And we went there. Children saw us coming, and called their father. And the peasant came out. It was maybe 8,000 feet high, a mountain village. He came out when we approached. And I greeted him in Catalan. The man's eye were big.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, How come you speak Spanish? I said, I lived in Spanish-- in Spain, in Barcelona for many years. And I have a good friend here-- a very influential men. He is the representative from International Red Cross in Figueres. His name is Juan Dolpho. Oh, I know Juan Dolpho!

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, then please, let us stay here. He said, this is a dangerous place here. The Guardia Civil-- that is the Spanish police-- they are-- they are patrolling here every single day. But OK, come. But you don't come out of that room, the back room that I have. It was a wide bed, more than a king size. It was king and queen size in one. And all five of us, we were lying in that bed. We stayed for two nights. I asked him to send his son down to Macanet and to telephone Mr. Dolpho and tell them that we had crossed the Pyrenees, and that we are in Spain. The boy came back in the evening and said, he said that you should come next morning to Macanet, to that-and-that hotel-- restaurant. And he will be coming there. So he guided us down to that village. And there I met Dolpho.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the same man who had come to see you in Perpignan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perpignan. We had had business dealings for several years when we still lived in Spain. And he had come to Holland. We had done business while I was in Holland. And my brother had practically saved his son's life when the Republicans were fighting Franco. They-- the Dolphos were anti-Republican. And they were looking for his son. And my brother Bob took him into our apartment and was hiding him. Otherwise, they would have shot him to death, the Republicans. So he owed us a gratitude.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, who are those two others? You were talking in Perpignan. I saw you and-- and your friend. Now where are these others? So I said, they are two other friends. He said, but I cannot make arrangements for five. That is too much. They are four-- four men. I said, listen, either all five of us or-- or none of us. I won't leave my friends here.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I'm going to talk to the jefe de policia, the police chief of that little village. He came back and said, it's arranged. But you will be in a prison for a short time. And then they send you on, which was a lie. He had lied to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was fine. So we went to-- to the police chief. He took his name. And-- and we were in a truck taken to Figueres, the first city in Spain. We were put into the police prison. And because the most important man in Figueres was our friend Juan Dolpho, we were treated like special guests. Dolpho sent us food, milk, chocolate, and other things, and-- and blankets, and pillows. So we were quite comfortable, to the envy of the other inmates there.

speaker: interviewee
text: After five days approximately, the head of the police station came to me and said, your wife will have to go to Girona. I said, what's Girona? To the prison, the women's prison. She cannot stay here. This is-- this is only for men. So my-- my heart ached when I said-- had to say goodbye. And [? Dottie ?] was heartbroken that she was separating to send to Girona to a prison. We corresponded. And he now and then came and gave me a report from her.

speaker: interviewee
text: After four weeks, we-- we were informed that we would be sent to the Campo de Concentracion Miranda de Ebro, which was in the northwestern part of Spain, almost close to the Atlantic Ocean. And because, under the Geneva Convention, Spain must intern men in military age until the end of the war. We arrived at Miranda de Ebro on my birthday, on the 23rd of December, 1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The camp was originally a camp for anti-Fascists who had been interned there-- 500 of them-- for-- by Franco. Now, there were no more Spaniards there. But instead of 500, there were 2,400 inmates-- French, Polish mostly. Then Dutch and-- and other nationalities, including a few Americans and British escaped-- who had escaped from France. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We tried by all means to find a way to get out. And I started immediately trying to convince the doctor, who-- a camp doctor-- everything was military-- a military doctor, to convince him that I had a broken hip, that I had fallen in the-- in the mountains, and I was in pain. And he-- he barely took notice of it, scribbled something. And that was it. But [? Dottie, ?] in the meantime, worked in-- had got-- after she was liberated from the prison after a few weeks, and was in residence force-- that is forced residence in Caldes de Malevella-- she was sent on to Madrid. Somehow, she wrote a letter to the-- to the wife of the ambassador, who was very impressed by this kind of letter, called her, and took a liking to [? Dottie. ?] And [? Dottie ?] ask her to please try to help me to get out of the camp. That was only possible if I was declared unfit for military service. And for that purpose, I would have to be sent to a military hospital in-- it's a place nearby, and examined by four doctors. If they found that I-- that I was unfit for military service, I would be released, and could go to Madrid. She-- the wife of the ambassador, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through the influence she had with Spanish high government officials, achieved it in a relatively short time. And-- and it must have been about the middle of March, I was suddenly called to come to the office. And there, they told me, you will be sent to-- to a military hospital in Vitoria. That was the name of the town. I was examined there by a lieutenant doctor. And I somehow started a kind of kinship with him, and convinced him that-- that I was in great pain, that I had fallen in the Pyrenees, and I had-- I had formerly already had an accident, and it hurt, and et cetera. He said, I have to measure your legs. Only if one leg is shorter than the other, then-- then you will be declared unfit for military service. You will-- you will first have to be x-rayed. Now, the nuns-- the nurses were all nuns. And when I was taken to the x-ray laboratory, an old-fashioned thing, she said to me, you lie down now. I have to go outside. And you lie still. As soon as she turned around to leave the room, I twisted my diaphragm so that one leg was shorter than the other. And they took the x-rays. He came in, the lieutenant, next morning, and said, let me measure your-- your two legs. He measured. And he found that the left that was two centimeters shorter than the right. How that is, I don't know. But that's what he found. A day later, I was called to the-- to the room of the director and several other doctors. I spoke only French, and didn't let on that I understood every single word. And I heard the director, who was a colonel, say, I think this guy is faking. Let's see. I'm going to ask him to walk up and down. Let's see how he gets up from the chair. Well, I faked it. With difficulty, I got up and walked, of course, limping. Then he said, tell him to-- to-- a priest was there as a-- as a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Interpreter?

speaker: interviewee
text: --interpreter The priest said to me, the director wants you to lie down on that thing. So I climbed-- I climbed up. And he tried to move my legs. I started yelling like crazy, and-- and held his hand. So the lieutenant said, Colonel, you see that this man is in terrible pain. In short, it-- I succeeded. A day later, I was sent back. And the priest, with a-- with a smile, said to me, you have nothing to worry. So I knew it was-- it was OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Dottie ?] found out that the ambassador had been ordered by the Queen Wilhelmina in London to visit the camp to convince himself of the state of health of the inmates. And she had the chutzpah to ask the-- the Mrs. [INAUDIBLE] if the ambassador and the First Secretary go there, could they perhaps take back my husband, who has just been freed? She said, sure, I'm going to tell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a call. I should come to the office and pack my stuff. And I was leaving. Had to sign out. Got my-- got some things back. Was introduced to the ambassador and to the First Secretary in his limousine. And we drove back to Madrid. I was sitting in the back seat next to the-- to the First Secretary, also from Dutch nobility. And he-- he asked me, tell me your story. And I told him the whole story of our flight and how I managed to-- to cheat those people. He found it wonderful. Then he heard that I spoke five languages. He said, you have been in Spain. You are fluent in Spanish. I said yes. You know the Spaniards. I said, yes, I think I know them. He said, we can use someone like you at the embassy and the consulate to negotiate with the Dutch authorities. You see, Dolf, the wind is turning. The Americans and the British troops are driving the Germans back [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH] is fleeing. The tide of the war has turned.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said before how you cheated those people, what did you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by faking that my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that my-- my diaphragm was crooked, that my one leg was shorter than the other, and I didn't speak Spanish, and all that. That I meant-- meant with it. And so he offered me a job at the-- at the embassy, or the consulate-- either one. But he said, first, you have to rest a while. I started working at the consulate, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and helped liberate Dutch men and a few women from prisons all over Spain-- and was very successful. I got an official employ, and a very good salary. And all monies that had been advanced to us by the Dutch government was struck out as past salary, which was a huge sum. [? Dottie ?] had been operated, in the meantime, on-- on appendicitis. That was also included. So all that money, all of a sudden, was struck down.
